## Check Stock Revenue by Country: Importance in 2026
URL: https://metricshour.com/blog/how-to-check-any-stocks-revenue-by-country-and-why-it-matters-more-than-ever-in-2026
Date: 2026-03-29 | Category: data

Stock Revenue by Country is vital for investors in 2026, as company performance heavily relies on global economies like China, Japan, and Germany.

When you buy Apple stock, you are not just investing in a Cupertino-based tech company. You are placing a $72.4 billion bet on the Chinese economy, a $24.1 billion bet on Japan, and an $18.8 billion bet on Germany. As of NVIDIA's FY2024 (ending January 2024), buying NVIDIA meant taking an $11.6 billion exposure to Taiwan and a $10.4 billion exposure to China—two of the most geopolitically sensitive markets on Earth.

This is geographic revenue exposure, and it is the single most overlooked risk factor in modern investing.

Most investors check a stock's P/E ratio, revenue growth, and earnings estimates before buying. Almost nobody checks where that revenue actually comes from. In 2026, with an active military conflict in the Middle East, ongoing U.S.-China trade tensions, and tariffs reshaping global supply chains, that oversight can be costly.

What Is Geographic Revenue Exposure?

Every publicly traded company in the United States is required to disclose where its revenue comes from in its annual 10-K filing with the SEC. These filings break down revenue by geographic segment, showing exactly which countries and regions contribute to a company's top line.

This data is not just a formality. It is a map of a company's vulnerabilities. Understanding this map allows an investor to identify three distinct types of risk:

1.  Macroeconomic Risk: A company deriving 40% of its sales from Europe is highly sensitive to a recession in the Eurozone. A slowdown in consumer spending in Germany or France will directly impact that company's top line, regardless of how well the U.S. economy is performing.

2.  Currency Risk: When a U.S. company sells products in Japan, it earns revenue in yen. That yen must be converted back to U.S. dollars for financial reporting. If the dollar strengthens against the yen, each yen earned translates into fewer dollars, depressing reported revenue and earnings. For a company like Nike, with over 55% of its revenue from outside North America as of FY2024, currency fluctuations are a constant variable.

3.  Geopolitical & Regulatory Risk: This is the most acute risk in 2026. A government can impose tariffs, enact sanctions, or implement new regulations that favor local competitors. The U.S. government's restrictions on advanced semiconductor sales to China directly threatened a significant portion of NVIDIA's business, forcing the company to develop lower-spec chips specifically for that market.

The problem is that this data is buried deep inside dense financial documents on SEC EDGAR. Most retail investors never see it. And most financial platforms, including popular tools like TradingView, Yahoo Finance, and Robinhood, do not surface it in a usable way.

That is where geographic revenue analysis becomes critical. By mapping a company's revenue to specific countries, investors can understand how macroeconomic events, currency fluctuations, tariffs, and geopolitical conflicts directly affect the stocks they own.

Real Examples: Where the Biggest Stocks Earn Their Money

Let's look at some of the most widely held stocks and their actual geographic revenue breakdown, sourced from their latest SEC 10-K filings.

Apple. Revenue by Country (FY2024)

| Country | Revenue Share | Revenue |
|---------|--------------|---------------|
| United States | 38.1% | $146.0 billion |
| China | 18.9% | $72.4 billion |
| Japan | 6.3% | $24.1 billion |
| Germany | 4.9% | $18.8 billion |
| United Kingdom | 4.9% | $18.8 billion |
| France | 3.7% | $14.2 billion |
| South Korea | 3.1% | $11.9 billion |
| Netherlands | 2.5% | $9.6 billion |
| Australia | 2.3% | $8.8 billion |
| Canada | 2.1% | $8.0 billion |
| India | 1.6% | $6.1 billion |

What this means: Nearly 19 percent of Apple's revenue, over $72 billion, comes from China. Every tariff escalation, every yuan depreciation, and every regulatory shift from Beijing flows directly into Apple's earnings. When the U.S. Imposed new tariffs on Chinese goods in early 2026, Apple's stock dropped 4 percent in a single session. Investors who understood the geographic exposure saw it coming.

Full breakdown: metricshour.com/stocks/[AAPL]

NVIDIA. Revenue by Country (FY2024)

| Country | Revenue Share | Revenue |
|---------|--------------|---------------|
| United States | 44% | $26.8 billion |
| Taiwan | 19% | $11.6 billion |
| China | 17% | $10.4 billion |
| Singapore | 16% | $9.7 billion |
| South Korea | 2% | $1.2 billion |
| Germany | 0.8% | $487 million |
| Japan | 0.8% | $487 million |
| India | 0.4% | $244 million |

What this means: NVIDIA's revenue is concentrated in some of the most geopolitically sensitive regions on the planet. Taiwan (19%) sits at the center of U.S.-China tensions. China (17%) faces ongoing chip export restrictions. When Jensen Huang announced NVIDIA would restart H200 chip shipments to China in early 2024, the stock moved 3 percent, because investors who track geographic revenue understood the $10.4 billion at stake.

Full breakdown: metricshour.com/stocks/[NVDA]

Tesla. Revenue by Country (FY2024)

| Country | Revenue Share | Revenue |
|---------|--------------|---------------|
| United States | 50% | $48.4 billion |
| China | 22% | $21.3 billion |
| Germany | 5% | $4.8 billion |
| Norway | 3% | $2.9 billion |
| United Kingdom | 2.5% | $2.4 billion |
| Australia | 2.5% | $2.4 billion |
| Canada | 2% | $1.9 billion |
| France | 2% | $1.9 billion |

What this means: While half of Tesla's revenue comes from the U.S., a massive 22% slice depends on China. This is not just about sales; it is about production. The Shanghai Gigafactory is one of Tesla's most efficient plants. Any disruption there, whether from local competition from BYD or a shift in policy from Beijing, presents a direct threat to Tesla's global operations and profitability.

Full breakdown: metricshour.com/stocks/[TSLA]

Microsoft. Revenue by Country (FY2024)

| Country | Revenue Share | Revenue |
|---------|--------------|---------------|
| United States | 51.2% | $117.8 billion |
| United Kingdom | 4.5% | $10.4 billion |
| Germany | 4.1% | $9.4 billion |
| Japan | 3.8% | $8.7 billion |
| China | 2.9% | $6.7 billion |
| Canada | 2.7% | $6.2 billion |
| France | 2.5% | $5.8 billion |

What this means: Microsoft appears more insulated, with just over half its revenue from the U.S. and a relatively small 2.9% from China. The risk here is more subtle. A significant portion of its international revenue comes from Azure cloud services. This business is subject to data sovereignty laws, which dictate where a country's data can be stored and processed. A new privacy law in the United Kingdom or a regulatory change in Germany could require costly changes to its data center operations, affecting margins on its fastest-growing segment.

Full breakdown: metricshour.com/stocks/[MSFT]

Beyond Revenue: The Supply Chain Blind Spot

Revenue exposure is only half the picture. The other half is operational and supply chain exposure. A company might generate 90% of its revenue in the United States but be completely dependent on a single factory in Vietnam or a key component supplier in Taiwan.

This data is harder to quantify, as companies are not required to disclose their full list of suppliers. But the risk is just as real.

Consider Apple again. Its $72.4 billion in Chinese revenue is one risk. Its manufacturing concentration with partners like Foxconn in China is a second, distinct risk. Its reliance on Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company (TSM) for the advanced chips in every iPhone is a third. A conflict in the Taiwan Strait would disrupt Apple's ability to produce its core products, affecting 100% of its revenue, not just the 19% it earns from China.

This principle applies across industries. Automakers depend on parts from Germany and Japan. Pharmaceutical companies rely on active ingredients manufactured in India and China. Analyzing revenue by country is the first step; thinking critically about the operational footprint is the second.

How to Analyze Geographic Risk in Your Portfolio

Applying this data does not require complex financial modeling. It requires a systematic approach to risk assessment.

Step 1: Audit Your Holdings.
Take your top five or ten largest positions. For each stock, find its geographic revenue breakdown. You can search the company's latest 10-K filing for "geographic information" or use a financial data platform that aggregates this information.

Step 2: Aggregate Your Exposure.
Create a simple spreadsheet. List your stocks in the rows and key countries or regions (China, Taiwan, Eurozone, Japan) in the columns. For each stock, multiply its portfolio weight by its revenue percentage from that country.

Example:
   Your portfolio is 10% [AAPL] and 5% [TSLA].
   [AAPL] gets 18.9% of revenue from China.
   [TSLA] gets 22% of revenue from China.
   Your effective portfolio exposure to China is (10%  18.9%) + (5%  22%) = 1.89% + 1.1% = 2.99%.

Summing this across all your holdings will reveal if your portfolio is unintentionally concentrated in a single, high-risk region.

Step 3: Stress-Test Your Assumptions.
Once you know your exposure, ask targeted questions. What happens to your portfolio if the U.S. and China enter a full-blown trade war? What is the impact of a 10% devaluation of the Euro? Thinking through these scenarios based on real data transforms abstract geopolitical news into a concrete portfolio risk you can manage. You might decide to trim a position with heavy exposure or diversify into companies with a more domestic focus.

Ignoring this data in 2026 is like driving a car without looking at the fuel gauge. You might be fine for a while, but you risk running empty at the worst possible moment.

---

## Starbucks China Revenue: $3.5B & Luckin's Impact
URL: https://metricshour.com/blog/starbucks-china-revenue-percentage
Date: 2026-03-28 | Category: markets

Starbucks China Revenue reached $3-3.5 billion in FY2023, solidifying its position as the second-largest market and analyzing Luckin Coffee's competitive

Starbucks generated approximately $3.0–3.5 billion from China in fiscal year 2023, roughly 9–10% of its $36 billion global revenue. China is Starbucks' second-largest market by store count and revenue, with approximately 6,800 locations and an a store count that has grown past 7,000 as of 2025. The numbers look like a growth story. The underlying economics are under pressure.

The Direct Ownership Model: Why This Matters

Most global QSR brands license their China operations. McDonald's and KFC sold their China businesses to franchisees; what appears on their income statements is royalty and fee income, not consolidated restaurant revenue. Starbucks is the exception. It owns and operates its China business outright, which means every yuan of revenue, every margin contraction, and every capex dollar is fully consolidated into Starbucks' global P&L. When comparable store sales decline in China, it shows up in Starbucks' earnings, not as a footnote in a licensed partner's disclosure.

This matters for investors because it makes Starbucks' financial results more directly sensitive to China dynamics than its QSR peers. In FY2023, China comparable store sales declined approximately 3%. That is a demand-side signal: existing stores are serving fewer customers or generating less per transaction. Starbucks is still opening new stores at pace, which masks the per-store deterioration in aggregate revenue growth, but the comp-store trend is what indicates whether the unit economics are holding.

What Luckin Has Done to the Market

Luckin Coffee went from near-bankruptcy after its 2020 accounting fraud to China's largest coffee chain by store count in 2023, surpassing Starbucks at approximately 9,000 locations. Its product quality has improved materially. Its app is widely regarded as better than Starbucks' local offering. And its pricing is structurally different: Luckin's average drink is approximately ¥15–20 ; Starbucks' average is ¥35–45. COTTI Coffee, founded by a Luckin co-founder, has added further price competition in the lower tier.

The effect is a market bifurcation. Chinese consumers increasingly use Luckin for everyday coffee and treat Starbucks as an occasional or social experience. This changes the frequency of Starbucks visits, and frequency is what drives comp-store sales. A brand that becomes less habitual and more occasional in a market faces structural volume pressure regardless of store count.

The issue is not product quality. Starbucks' core offer in China has not deteriorated. The issue is the price gap between daily-use coffee and premium coffee has widened, and Luckin has firmly claimed the daily-use tier. Starbucks built its China business partly on converting Chinese consumers to coffee culture; Luckin arrived and captured the habit, leaving Starbucks with the occasion. That segmentation shift is difficult to reverse with marketing alone because the price differential is not a promotion, it is Luckin's permanent unit economics model.

Starbucks' response has included app improvements, localized menu development, and loyalty program expansion. These are the right responses but they address symptom management, not the underlying positioning shift. Opening more stores increases total revenue from new units but does not fix the comp-store trajectory. A company that grows top line through new unit count while comp-store sales decline is effectively running two different businesses simultaneously, one that is expanding and one that is contracting, and the net result depends on which rate dominates.

Metric
Starbucks China (FY2023)
Luckin Coffee (2023 est.)

Store count
~6,800
~9,000

Average drink price
~¥35–45 
~¥15–20 

FY2023 comp-store sales
~–3%
Strong growth 

China P&L in parent financials
Fully consolidated
Standalone listed company

The Potential Licensing Pivot

In 2024, Starbucks disclosed it was exploring strategic options for its China business, including a potential licensing or partnership model, similar to how it runs Japan through a licensed entity. If this pivot happens, the financial impact would be significant: China's gross revenue would exit Starbucks' consolidated top line and be replaced by royalty and fee income. Revenue would drop; margin quality would improve; capital exposure to China would reduce substantially.

There is a precedent: Yum Brands spun off its China operations as Yum China Holdings in 2016. That removed KFC and Pizza Hut China from Yum's consolidated P&L entirely and created a separately listed entity. Yum Brands now collects royalties; Yum China operates the restaurants. An investor reading only Yum Brands' revenue would see no China revenue, but the China business exists and is substantial. If Starbucks follows a similar path, the headline revenue decline would be structural, not an operational deterioration.

For investors, the right metric to watch is not aggregate China revenue but comparable store sales, operating margin trend, and any strategic announcement on ownership structure. A 9–10% revenue line that is contracting in per-store economics is a different risk profile than 9–10% that is growing healthily. The store count expansion obscures this distinction in the headline numbers.

The broader implication is that the direct ownership model, which gave Starbucks full upside from China's coffee market expansion over the past decade, now also gives it full downside exposure to the competitive compression. The licensing structure that Yum adopted in 2016 looks increasingly prescient in retrospect. Starbucks entered China when the risk was missing the growth; it is now evaluating whether to exit full ownership because the risk has shifted to absorbing the losses from a market it can no longer dominate on its own terms.

MetricsHour tracks geographic revenue for consumer companies including Starbucks' China segment. The China country overview provides macroeconomic context for the consumer environment, and the stock screener lets investors compare China revenue exposure across global consumer brands.

---

## GM China Restructure $2.9B Charge Auto Market Shift
URL: https://metricshour.com/blog/automotive-companies-china-revenue
Date: 2026-03-28 | Category: trade

GM's China restructure marks the end of an era for Western automakers, as the $2.9B non-cash charge signals a lasting shift in the auto market.

General Motors recorded a ~$2.9 billion non-cash charge in Q4 2023 to restructure its China joint venture operations. This is the accounting entry that marks the end of a two-decade assumption: that China would be a reliable profit engine for Western automakers. It was, and then it wasn't.

The JV Structure: Why GM's China Revenue Was Always Invisible

Western automakers entered China through 50/50 joint ventures with state-owned partners. The JV requirement, which China has since partially relaxed, meant these companies never consolidated China revenue into their reported top lines. GM's China sales appear as "equity income from JVs", not as revenue. In peak years (2015–2017), GM's JVs sold approximately 4 million vehicles annually in China, making it GM's largest single market by volume. None of that showed in consolidated revenue. By 2023, JV sales had fallen to approximately 2.1 million vehicles as BYD, Li Auto, and Chinese EV brands took share. The equity income that once supplemented GM's earnings is now negligible.

Investors who screened GM by "China revenue %" saw zero and concluded minimal exposure. The actual exposure, via equity income, capital committed to JVs, and strategic dependency on a market that absorbed 4 million vehicles at peak, was significant. The $2.9 billion charge quantifies the cost of that misreading.

Ford: Direct Revenue, Direct Decline

Ford reports China revenue directly, which makes the decline visible. China represented approximately 4–5% of Ford's total FY2023 revenue, down sharply from a ~5% market share in 2016 to ~2% in 2023. In response, Ford's China operations have shifted focus from selling vehicles to Chinese consumers toward using Chinese manufacturing capacity for export. Lincoln and Transit units built in China for other markets. This is a retreat with a manufacturing continuation, not a full exit, but the revenue story is contracting.

The Broader Western Market Share Collapse

GM's trajectory is not an isolated case. The collective market share of Western OEM brands in China declined from approximately 50% in 2015 to roughly 30% by end of 2023, with the trend continuing into 2024–2025. The primary driver is China's domestic EV market. BYD, Li Auto, AITO/Huawei, NIO, and Xpeng have moved from niche players to mainstream volume competitors in under five years. They compete on software-defined features, local brand preference, and price points that Western brands cannot match without fundamentally restructuring their cost base. Chinese consumers are not just buying cheaper cars, they are buying products with more relevant features for their use case: range, fast-charging, over-the-air software updates, and integration with Chinese app sectors.

Tesla: The Strongest Western Position. Under Pressure

Tesla at ~22% China revenue is the outlier among Western automotive brands. Its Shanghai Gigafactory gives it local production economics and supply chain proximity. Its EV-native positioning meant it was competing in China's growth segment from the start, rather than defending a legacy ICE share. But BYD surpassed Tesla as China's top EV seller in 2023, and BYD now competes with Tesla in every price segment with products tailored specifically to Chinese consumer preferences. Tesla's China position is resilient relative to GM and Ford, it is not immune to domestic competition.

Auto Suppliers: A Different China Story

The OEM retreat does not necessarily translate to supplier retreat. Auto component and technology companies sell into the entire Chinese production base, including the domestic Chinese brands that are taking share from Western OEMs. BorgWarner generates approximately 25% of its revenue from China. Its exposure runs across ICE and EV drivetrains; as Chinese EV brands grow, their demand for electrification components partly offsets declining ICE volumes. Aptiv at ~21% China revenue supplies connected vehicle electronics and benefits from the technology content expansion in Chinese OEM models. Gentex at ~19% supplies auto-dimming mirrors and camera systems to Chinese OEMs.

The supplier dynamic is distinct: their China revenue is not brand-dependent. BYD buying BorgWarner components is still BorgWarner revenue, regardless of whether Ford's market share is declining. For investors, this is the key bifurcation in automotive China exposure. OEM revenue is tied to Western brand competitiveness; supplier revenue is tied to total Chinese production volume.

Company
Ticker
China Revenue / Structure
Key Dynamic

General Motors
GM
JV equity income 
JV sales 4M → 2.1M; $2.9B restructuring charge Q4 2023

Ford
F
~4–5% consolidated revenue
Market share 5% → 2%; pivoting to manufacturing-for-export

Tesla
TSLA
~22% consolidated revenue
Strongest Western EV position; direct BYD competition in all segments

BorgWarner
BWA
~25% consolidated revenue
Sells to all OEMs including Chinese brands; EV transition changes mix

Aptiv
APTV
~21% consolidated revenue
Connected vehicle electronics; benefits from Chinese OEM tech expansion

Gentex
GNTX
~19% consolidated revenue
Auto-dimming mirrors and cameras to Chinese OEMs

The investment implication of the OEM-supplier split is practical. An investor who wants automotive China revenue exposure without betting on whether Ford or GM can recover their brand share in China can access that exposure through supplier names like BorgWarner or Aptiv, whose revenue depends on total Chinese production volume rather than Western brand loyalty. Conversely, an investor who wants to reduce automotive China exposure should focus on OEM positions where the JV equity income line or the directly consolidated China revenue is material.

MetricsHour tracks geographic revenue for automotive companies, including the JV-structure nuances where relevant. The China country overview provides context on vehicle market dynamics, and the stock screener lets investors compare China exposure across OEMs and suppliers side by side.

---

## Pharma Stocks Most Exposed to China Revenue
URL: https://metricshour.com/blog/pharma-stocks-china-revenue-exposure
Date: 2026-03-28 | Category: trade

Pharma stocks most exposed to China revenue are highlighted, focusing on AstraZeneca, which earns 13% or $7.8 billion from China, its largest market and

AstraZeneca generates approximately 13% of its revenue from China, around $7.8 billion in 2023. That makes China its largest market outside the US, and it is the highest China revenue share among the major global pharmaceutical groups. In 2024, one of AstraZeneca's China executives was detained in a compliance investigation related to unauthorized drug imports. That combination, highest exposure and highest-profile compliance incident, frames the range of risk in pharma's China story.

How China Pharma Revenue Actually Works

China is the world's second-largest pharmaceutical market, and its patient population for chronic conditions, cancer, diabetes, cardiovascular disease, is the largest in absolute terms. Yet most global pharma companies report China as a smaller percentage of revenue than its market size would suggest. Three structural factors explain this gap: the NMPA approval lag, the NRDL pricing mechanism, and the biosimilar competitive environment. Understanding all three is necessary to read the China revenue figures correctly.

Two mechanisms shape every drug company's China revenue trajectory. First, NMPA approval: each drug requires a separate Chinese regulatory review, which typically runs one to three years behind Western markets. A company with a strong pipeline of NMPA-pending drugs has latent China revenue that the current numbers don't reflect. Second, NRDL inclusion: to access the majority of Chinese patients, a drug needs to be reimbursed. Annual NRDL negotiations typically force price cuts of 50–80% off initial list price. The tradeoff is volume, reimbursed drugs see dramatic patient access expansion. A drug company that accepts the price cut often ends up with more total revenue from China than one that holds on price and serves only out-of-pocket patients.

These two mechanisms mean China revenue figures in current filings are a floor, not a ceiling. The relevant question is what's in the NMPA pipeline and whether the NRDL pricing is sustainable for each company's portfolio.

Company-by-Company Breakdown

AstraZeneca at ~13% China revenue is in a category of its own among Western pharma. Its broad oncology, cardiovascular, and respiratory portfolio has accumulated approvals across multiple NRDL cycles. CEO Pascal Soriot has been partly based in China as a signal of strategic commitment. The 2024 compliance investigation, involving alleged unauthorized imports of cancer drugs for patients outside approved protocols, is a reminder that operating at this scale in China carries regulatory and legal risk that is qualitatively different from tariff risk.

Pfizer at ~9% China revenue peaked higher during the COVID vaccine cycle and has since contracted. Its base China business in oncology, vaccines, and cardiovascular medicine is stable, with new NMPA approvals providing incremental growth. China is a top-three market for Pfizer outside the US.

Merck at ~7–8% China revenue has its China growth story tied almost entirely to Keytruda. The NMPA has progressively approved Keytruda across multiple cancer indications, and each new approval is an incremental revenue access. NRDL inclusion for Keytruda came with steep price reductions but generated significant patient volume in return. China is a strategic priority for Merck's oncology pipeline specifically.

Eli Lilly at ~5–6% China revenue has the most compelling structural growth case: China has approximately 140 million diabetic patients, the world's largest diabetic population, and Lilly's GLP-1 drugs address this directly. If Mounjaro receives NMPA approval and NRDL inclusion, it would represent a step-change in Lilly's China revenue, even after the price haircut.

Johnson & Johnson at ~5–7% China revenue carries exposure across both its pharmaceutical and MedTech segments. Oncology drugs, surgical robotics, and orthopedic devices each have their own NMPA approval pathways and procurement dynamics. The MedTech side faces China's government-driven volume-based procurement program, which imposes price cuts on medical devices similar to NRDL for drugs.

Bristol-Myers Squibb at ~4–5% has its China exposure concentrated in oncology. Opdivo and Revlimid. Both have NMPA approvals; both have been through NRDL negotiations. BMY's China revenue is essentially a function of oncology access dynamics.

AbbVie at ~3–4% has the lowest China share of the group, partly because Humira never achieved the China penetration it did in Western markets. Biosimilar competitors arrived in China earlier and more aggressively than in the US, limiting Humira's market share. AbbVie's newer immunology drugs are growing in China, but the Humira-sized revenue that Western investors might expect from a drug of that profile was never there.

Company
Ticker
China Revenue %
Key China Driver

AstraZeneca
AZN
~13%
Broad portfolio, NRDL inclusion; compliance risk highlighted 2024

Pfizer
PFE
~9%
Oncology, vaccines; post-COVID normalization underway

Merck
MRK
~7–8%
Keytruda NMPA approvals expanding across indications

Johnson & Johnson
JNJ
~5–7%
Pharma + MedTech 

Eli Lilly
LLY
~5–6%
GLP-1 pipeline ; diabetes epidemic driver

Bristol-Myers Squibb
BMY
~4–5%
Opdivo and Revlimid; oncology-focused exposure

AbbVie
ABBV
~3–4%
Newer immunology drugs; Humira limited by early biosimilar competition

The biosimilar dynamic is worth isolating as a distinct risk factor. For biologics, large-molecule drugs like Humira. China's regulatory environment allowed biosimilar competition to arrive faster than in the US or Europe. Chinese manufacturers produced biosimilar versions of adalimumab, trastuzumab, and other key biologics earlier and more cheaply than Western markets anticipated. Any pharma company with a flagship biologic generating significant revenue should be stress-tested against this biosimilar timeline, not just the Western-market schedule.

The pharma sector's China exposure is more dynamic than most other sectors because it is driven by a discrete pipeline of regulatory approvals rather than macroeconomic demand. A single NMPA approval can add hundreds of millions to China revenue; a single NRDL price negotiation can compress margins significantly. MetricsHour tracks geographic revenue breakdowns for pharmaceutical companies, including China-specific data where disclosed. The China country page provides macroeconomic context, and the stock screener lets investors compare China exposure levels across the sector.

---

## S&P 500 Zero China Revenue Stocks Domestic List
URL: https://metricshour.com/blog/sp500-stocks-zero-china-revenue
Date: 2026-03-28 | Category: markets

S&P 500 Zero China Revenue Stocks: These domestically-focused companies offer full portfolio protection from US-China trade risk and tariffs.

If you screen S&P 500 stocks by China revenue, the high-exposure names get most of the attention. The other end of the spectrum is harder to find, and more useful for investors who want to isolate portfolios from US-China trade risk entirely. Roughly eight sectors in the S&P 500 produce companies with zero or structurally de minimis China revenue, and the reason is consistent across all of them: their businesses physically cannot operate in China, or the economics make it irrelevant.

The distinction between "zero by design" and "zero by accident" matters. A domestic waste company will never generate China revenue, not because it chose to avoid China, but because waste collection requires trucks, landfills, and municipal contracts that are jurisdiction-locked. A tech company with zero China revenue in 2024 might have 15% in 2025 if a distribution deal or licensing arrangement changes. For a tariff-driven portfolio screen, only the structural zero is durable.

Waste and Environmental Services

Waste Management and Republic Services are the clearest cases. Both companies generate their entire revenue from North American waste collection, transfer, recycling, and landfill operations. WM generates roughly $20 billion annually; RSG around $14 billion. Their customer contracts are municipal or commercial, their assets are fixed infrastructure, and their service delivery is physically bounded by geography. There is no version of their business model that generates a China revenue line.

Utilities

Electric and gas utilities are regulated geographic monopolies. NextEra Energy, Duke Energy, and Southern Company serve defined US service territories under state and federal regulatory frameworks. Their revenue comes from US ratepayers, their capital base is US grid infrastructure, and international expansion, let alone China, is structurally outside their operating model. The regulatory structure that creates their competitive moat also creates their geographic ceiling.

Healthcare Services

Hospital operators like HCA Healthcare and Tenet Healthcare are entirely US-based. HCA generates approximately $60 billion annually from its US hospital and ambulatory network, zero from international operations. The US healthcare payment system is what their entire business is built around; it is untransferable to China's completely different system. This is zero-China exposure by business design, not by choice.

Domestic Retail

Dollar General and Dollar Tree operate US and Canada discount retail chains. Dollar General's ~$38 billion in annual revenue comes entirely from its US store base, predominantly serving rural and suburban markets. These businesses have no international footprint. Their competitive advantage, dense physical store networks in underserved communities, is inherently domestic and cannot be replicated elsewhere to generate China revenue.

Auto Aftermarket

AutoZone and O'Reilly Automotive sell auto parts to US DIY consumers and professional installers. Both companies operate in the Americas, primarily the US. Their revenue is tied to the aging US vehicle fleet and local service demand. Note that these companies source some products from China, so they carry tariff cost risk on the supply side, but their revenue is entirely domestic, meaning no Chinese retaliation or Chinese demand shift affects their top line.

Insurance and Telecom

Progressive and Allstate underwrite auto and home insurance in the US under state regulatory frameworks. T-Mobile and Verizon are US wireless carriers whose revenue comes from domestic subscribers. The regulatory environments for both sectors are jurisdiction-specific, insurance licensing is state-by-state, and wireless spectrum licenses are country-specific. Neither segment can generate China revenue without a completely different business model.

Homebuilders

D.R. Horton and Lennar are two of the largest US homebuilders. Their revenue comes from building and selling homes to US buyers in US markets. Land acquisition, construction, and sales are tied to local housing demand, zoning laws, and US mortgage financing. Like utilities and hospitals, this is an inherently domestic business with no viable path to China revenue.

Sector
Example Tickers
Why Zero China Revenue

Waste Management
WM, RSG
Physical service delivery; municipal contracts; jurisdiction-locked assets

Utilities
NEE, DUK, SO
Regulated geographic monopolies; US grid infrastructure only

Hospital Services
HCA, THC
US-only hospital networks; US payment system dependency

Domestic Retail
DG, DLTR
US-only store base; no international footprint

Auto Aftermarket
AZO, ORLY
US vehicle fleet focus; domestic parts distribution 

Insurance
PGR, ALL
US state-regulated policies; jurisdiction-specific underwriting

Wireless Telecom
TMUS, VZ
US spectrum licenses; domestic subscriber base

Homebuilders
DHI, LEN
US land, construction, and sales; domestic mortgage market

When Zero-China Stocks Make Sense in a Portfolio

The investment case for zero-China stocks is strongest when the primary concern is policy risk rather than economic risk. Tariff escalations, export controls, and retaliation campaigns affect companies with China revenue or China-sourced supply chains. None of those mechanisms can touch a domestic utility, a hospital operator, or a waste company. In a portfolio that already carries significant China exposure through technology or consumer holdings, adding positions in the sectors above provides a genuine diversification without the usual sector correlation.

The tradeoff is clear: zero China revenue means zero China risk, no tariff retaliation, no demand sensitivity to Chinese economic cycles, no compliance exposure. It also means zero participation in China's growth. That is the right tradeoff for some investors and the wrong one for others. The sectors above are not avoiding China; they are structurally incapable of being there.

One further distinction: some of these domestically-focused companies still source materials or products from China, auto aftermarket retailers, for instance, import Chinese-manufactured parts. That creates supply-side tariff exposure on their cost base even when their revenue is entirely domestic. Zero China revenue is not the same as zero China supply chain exposure. Investors who want full isolation from the US-China trade cycle need to check both sides of the ledger.

MetricsHour tracks geographic revenue for public companies, including the domestic-only cases where reported China revenue is zero. The stock screener lets investors compare revenue exposure by geography across the S&P 500, and the China country page provides the macroeconomic context for why that exposure level matters.

---

## Reshoring Stocks Cutting China Exposure
URL: https://metricshour.com/blog/reshoring-stocks-reducing-china-exposure
Date: 2026-03-28 | Category: trade

These reshoring stocks are actively cutting China supply chain exposure, as Nike and Apple shift production to Vietnam and India to cut tariff risk.

Nike sourced roughly 30% of its footwear from China a decade ago. As of FY2024, that number is ~18%, with Vietnam at ~50%. Apple's Greater China revenue has declined from ~25% of total sales in FY2015 to ~19% in FY2023 (FY2023 being the most recent complete year in the dataset). Qualcomm's China share dropped from above 60% to ~46% after the Huawei entity listing. These are three different types of China reduction, and conflating them leads to the wrong investment conclusion.

The distinction matters: revenue reduction is a demand-side shift. Chinese consumers buying less of a US brand, or export controls cutting off customers. This hits the top line directly. Supply chain reduction, moving manufacturing from China to India, Vietnam, or Mexico, is a cost-side shift that involves transition expenses but ultimately reduces tariff exposure. True reshoring means production returns to the US. Most of what's actually happening is friend-shoring to allied nations. The companies doing both simultaneously are in the most complex position.

Companies Where China Revenue Has Declined

Apple is the clearest large-cap example of a gradual revenue share reduction. Greater China peaked at ~25% of total revenue in FY2015 and registered ~19% in FY2023. The absolute dollar amount, roughly $72 billion, is still growing, but Apple's non-China business has expanded faster, diluting China's proportional weight. That is a different dynamic than a boycott-driven decline: it reflects a company growing globally while China growth normalizes.

Nike experienced the sharper version. Greater China revenues were tracking toward ~17% of total when the 2021 Xinjiang cotton controversy triggered Chinese consumer boycotts. Revenue fell to ~14–15% of total and has only partially recovered. This is the retaliation scenario playing out in real time, a Western brand losing Chinese market share to domestic competitors like Anta and Li-Ning not because of inferior product, but because of brand politics.

Qualcomm saw a forced reduction. Before the US entity list placement of Huawei in 2020, China represented over 60% of Qualcomm's revenue. Huawei alone was an ~$8 billion annual customer. The restriction cut that customer off. China is still ~46% of revenue as of FY2024 because Qualcomm's chips remain embedded in the rest of the Chinese handset industry, but the structural peak has passed.

Intel has moved from above 30% to ~27% China revenue as US export controls on advanced chips tighten and Chinese cloud and data center customers are pushed toward domestic alternatives. Las Vegas Sands, though a different sector, has reduced Macau's share from above 60% pre-COVID to ~40% in FY2023 (FY2023 being the most recent complete year in the dataset), primarily through the structural success of Marina Bay Sands in Singapore, a genuine geographic diversification of revenue, not a demand problem.

Companies Actively Reducing Supply Chain Dependence on China

Nike's supply chain shift is the most complete story in S&P 500 consumer goods. China was ~30% of footwear sourcing a decade ago; Vietnam has since become the primary manufacturing country at ~50%, with Indonesia at ~27%. China is now ~18%. This did not happen overnight, it took consistent sourcing decisions over roughly eight years, but it now provides meaningful insulation against import tariffs that hit China-origin goods.

Apple's supply chain diversification is real but earlier-stage. Tata Electronics in India is assembling iPhone 15 and 16 models; India's share of total iPhone output rose to approximately 15-20% through 2025, with Apple targeting 25%+ by 2027. Foxconn is also building India capacity. China still accounts for roughly 87–90% of iPhone assembly, which means Apple's transition costs are front-loaded and the tariff relief is still limited. The direction is clear; the scale is not yet sufficient.

Dell Technologies has shifted meaningful laptop and PC assembly to Vietnam and India over the past five years, reducing China's share of production materially. Hasbro and Mattel, once sourcing 60–70% of production from China, have diversified to Vietnam, India, and Mexico, driven by both tariff pressure and the narrowing of China's labor cost advantage over the past decade.

Semiconductor manufacturing reshoring sits in a category of its own. TSMC's $65 billion Arizona commitment, Samsung's Taylor, Texas facility, and Intel's $20 billion Ohio investment are not moving production out of mainland China, they are reducing dependence on Taiwan, which carries the same geopolitical risk profile. For US companies that rely on leading-edge chips, this reduces the single-point-of-failure risk that China-Taiwan tension creates.

Company
Ticker
China Exposure 
What's Shifting

Apple
AAPL
~25% revenue (FY2015) → ~19% (FY2023)
Revenue share declining; supply chain moving to India 

Nike
NKE
~17% revenue / ~30% sourcing → ~14–15% / ~18%
Revenue hit by 2021 boycott; sourcing shifted to Vietnam/Indonesia over decade

Qualcomm
QCOM
>60% revenue → ~46% (FY2023)
Forced revenue reduction. Huawei entity listing cut largest customer

Intel
INTC
>30% revenue → ~27% (FY2023)
Export controls pushing Chinese customers to domestic alternatives

Las Vegas Sands
LVS
>60% Macau revenue → ~40% (FY2023)
Deliberate geographic diversification via Marina Bay Sands 

Dell Technologies
DELL
China-dominant production → declining share
Assembly shifted to Vietnam, India over 5 years

Hasbro / Mattel
HAS / MAT
~60–70% China production → lower, undisclosed
Diversified to Vietnam, India, Mexico, tariff pressure + labor cost shift

The risk of partial friend-shoring is worth naming directly. A company that has moved 20% of production out of China still has 80% exposure. Tariffs apply to that 80%. The margin benefit of having a Vietnam factory is real but limited until the shift reaches the scale that fundamentally changes the company's landed-cost structure. Nike took eight years to get from 30% to 18% China sourcing, and it still has 18% to go. For companies earlier in that process, the transition cost is present and the tariff relief is not yet.

MetricsHour tracks geographic revenue breakdowns for public companies over time, making it possible to see which companies' China exposure is actually declining versus holding flat. The country pages for China and India provide macroeconomic context for both the outbound and inbound sides of this shift, and the stock screener lets investors compare geographic revenue across companies in the same sector.

---

## S&P 500 Companies Most Exposed to China Tariffs
URL: https://metricshour.com/blog/sp500-companies-most-exposed-china-tariffs
Date: 2026-03-28 | Category: trade

China tariffs impact S&P 500 companies in varied ways; understand the real risks for firms like Qualcomm and Apple, avoiding common investor mistakes.

Qualcomm earns roughly 46% of its revenue from China. Apple assembles approximately 90% of its iPhones there. Those are different risks, and conflating them is the most common mistake investors make when assessing China tariff exposure. The first is a revenue problem: if China retaliates against US tariffs or pressures domestic brands to avoid US suppliers, Qualcomm loses customers. The second is a cost problem: if the US raises import tariffs on Chinese-assembled goods, Apple's cost of goods sold rises. Some companies carry both.

Revenue Exposure: Companies That Sell to China

The semiconductor sector has the most concentrated China revenue exposure among S&P 500 companies, primarily because China manufactures the majority of the world's consumer electronics and has invested heavily in domestic chip production, all of which requires US equipment and components.

Qualcomm sits at the top at approximately 46% China revenue, driven by modem and RF chip sales to Chinese handset manufacturers. The Huawei restrictions have already demonstrated how quickly this revenue can be removed by US policy, and Chinese retaliation or domestic substitution would add a second lever. KLA Corporation at ~43% and Lam Research at ~37% supply inspection and etch equipment to Chinese fabs; their exposure is already under pressure from US export controls, with tariffs a potential additional constraint. Applied Materials is at ~32%, Broadcom at ~35%, and Intel at ~27%.

Beyond semiconductors, Apple generated approximately $72 billion from Greater China in FY2023, representing ~19% of group revenue. Tesla reported roughly 22% of its revenue from China in FY2024, with most of it produced locally at the Shanghai Gigafactory. Both face the retaliation vector: a Chinese government pressure campaign on US consumer brands, as seen previously with iPhones and Tesla vehicles, can move demand metrics without a single formal tariff.

Supply Chain Exposure: Companies That Source from China

US import tariffs on Chinese goods hit companies that manufacture in China or source components there, regardless of where those goods are sold. This is a COGS problem, not a revenue problem, and it shows up in gross margin before it shows up in the top line.

Apple is again the clearest case. With ~90% of iPhone assembly in China via Foxconn and Pegatron, a meaningful tariff on Chinese-assembled electronics hits Apple's production cost directly. India manufacturing through Tata is ramping but is years from replacing China's scale and component sector. The short-term cost absorption capacity is real but finite.

Nike still sources approximately 18% of its footwear from China, with Vietnam and Indonesia now holding larger shares after a multi-year diversification effort. That 18% is enough to be material against Nike's gross margins, particularly if tariffs apply uniformly to footwear categories where the production is harder to substitute quickly. Hasbro and Mattel have diversified meaningfully from their historical 50–70% China production levels but retain significant exposure, particularly for product lines requiring the specialized tooling and manufacturing infrastructure concentrated in Guangdong province. Retailers face a broader structural version of the same problem: Best Buy's consumer electronics assortment, televisions, laptops, peripherals, is heavily China-sourced across multiple vendor relationships, and Walmart estimates suggest 10–15% of total COGS trace back to Chinese origin, spread across thousands of SKUs rather than concentrated in any single category.

The Double-Exposure Names

Apple and Tesla are the two large-cap names that carry both forms of exposure simultaneously. Apple sells ~$72 billion into China and assembles the product there. A trade escalation that triggers Chinese consumer pressure on US brands and raises US import tariffs on Chinese goods would hit both the revenue line and the cost line in the same quarter. Tesla's Shanghai factory produces for both the domestic Chinese market and for export; its double exposure is somewhat more contained because much of its China production is sold in China rather than imported back to the US, but any Chinese government directive restricting Tesla sales, or retaliatory tariffs on imported Tesla components, would affect both sides.

Not all China exposure is equally sticky. Qualcomm's modem and RF chips are deeply embedded in Chinese handset designs, the engineering integration required to switch to an alternative supplier is measured in product cycles, not quarters. Semiconductor equipment from KLA, Lam, and Applied Materials is similarly irreplaceable on short timescales; Chinese fabs cannot qualify domestic alternatives for advanced process nodes fast enough to offset a near-term US supply restriction. In contrast, toy manufacturers and apparel brands can shift supply chains, the infrastructure exists in Vietnam, Bangladesh, and Mexico to absorb much of that production, even if the transition takes two to three years and compresses margins in the interim. The practical distinction matters for risk assessment: for the semiconductor names, China exposure is structural and the duration of impact is long. For consumer goods and retail, it is a cost and execution problem with a visible endpoint.

Exposure at a Glance

Company

Tariff Risk Type

MetricsHour tracks geographic revenue breakdowns for public companies, including China-specific disclosure where available. The country overview at China provides macroeconomic context, and the stock screener lets investors compare revenue exposure across geographies, useful for building a view on tariff sensitivity before trade policy changes force a reassessment.

## S&P 500 US Stocks Profiting from India Growth
URL: https://metricshour.com/blog/us-stocks-india-revenue-exposure
Date: 2026-03-28 | Category: markets

S&P 500 US stocks profiting from India growth often hide their revenues, but Cummins, Colgate-Palmolive, Mastercard, and Visa reveal their significant

Cummins earns roughly $1.1 billion a year from India. Colgate-Palmolive runs a majority-owned listed subsidiary there. Mastercard and Visa name it a top-5 growth market. Yet for most S&P 500 companies, India revenue sits buried inside an "Asia-Pacific" or "Rest of World" line. Finding US stocks with direct India revenue exposure requires reading past the macro story and into the actual filings.

Why India Revenue Is Hard to Read

India is the world's most populous country and its fastest-growing major economy, with GDP expanding at 6.5–7% annually. That macro story is well-known. What gets less attention is how few US multinationals actually break out India as a standalone revenue segment. Most bundle it with broader geographies, so the investor doing geographic revenue analysis has to distinguish between companies that treat India as a cost center. IT outsourcing, back-office, manufacturing for export, and those that generate actual domestic sales revenue there. The two look similar in corporate strategy slides. They are not the same in the income statement.

A second complication is that many technology and consulting companies headquartered in the US have enormous India headcounts, running engineering centers, support operations, and back-office functions there. That employment presence often gets conflated with India market exposure. Companies like Accenture, Cognizant, and Infosys employ hundreds of thousands in India. But those employees are producing services sold to US and European clients. India is the cost center, not the customer. For this analysis, the focus is strictly on companies where India is a source of revenue, not a location of labor.

For a full picture of India's macroeconomic environment, MetricsHour tracks key indicators at India.

Companies with Explicit India Revenue Disclosure

Cummins Inc. is the clearest case among large-cap US stocks. India represents approximately 10–12% of annual revenue, around $1.1 billion of its ~$10 billion total, and Cummins reports it as a standalone geographic segment. The company sells diesel and natural gas engines and power generation systems into India's industrial and infrastructure markets. Its India exposure is structural: it tracks directly to India's multi-year investment in roads, railways, manufacturing capacity, and power infrastructure.

Colgate-Palmolive derives an estimated 8–9% of total revenue from India, making it one of the highest India revenue shares among US consumer staples companies. It operates through Colgate-Palmolive India, a majority-owned subsidiary independently listed on India's National Stock Exchange. That listed structure provides an unusual level of transparency, investors can track the India business directly through the subsidiary's own filings, rather than relying on a bundled segment.

Significant Exposure Without Direct Disclosure

Mastercard and Visa do not break out India revenue but both explicitly identify India as a top-5 market by transaction volume growth. India's digital payments sector, driven by the Unified Payments Interface, is growing at roughly 25–30% annually in volume terms. More transactions mean higher processing fees and network usage for both networks, even if the rupee revenue per transaction is lower than in developed markets. Both companies describe India as a strategic priority in investor communications.

Honeywell International reports India within its broader emerging markets segment, with estimates placing India at 5–7% of group revenue, or approximately $1.8 billion. Honeywell's industrial portfolio, aerospace components, building technologies, energy infrastructure, aligns with India's sustained public infrastructure spending. Airport modernization and smart city development are two of its clearest demand drivers there.

3M Company discloses India within Asia-Pacific. India is estimated at 4–5% of group revenue. Its products, industrial adhesives, healthcare solutions, safety equipment, track India's manufacturing output and healthcare sector expansion.

Alphabet/Google does not report India revenue. Analyst estimates place it at 5–7% of global advertising revenue, supported by 500 million+ internet users and dominant Android and YouTube penetration. Monetization per user remains materially lower than in the US, Google India earns a fraction per impression of what it earns per impression in the US or Europe, which means the India number is large in volume and smaller in revenue than the user count implies. It also means the upside is real: as Indian digital ad spend per user rises toward regional averages, the revenue contribution grows without adding a single new user.

Apple has historically generated under 3% of revenue from India, but the trajectory has shifted. Local manufacturing via Foxconn and Tata has allowed Apple to price iPhones more competitively in-country while avoiding import duties. The opening of its first Indian retail stores in Mumbai and Delhi in 2023 established a direct consumer channel. Apple does not disaggregate India in its filings, but management has called it out as a priority market in recent earnings calls, a signal that its contribution to the top line is becoming harder to ignore.

How the Exposure Compares

Company
Ticker
India Revenue % 
Disclosure Method

Cummins Inc.
CMI
10–12%
Standalone segment

Colgate-Palmolive
CL
8–9%

Honeywell International
HON
5–7%
Within emerging markets segment

Alphabet / Google
GOOGL
5–7% 
Not disclosed

Not disclosed
Top-5 market by transaction volume growth

Strategic priority market

AAPL

Not disaggregated

The key risks attached to India exposure are consistent across most of these names: rupee depreciation reduces reported USD revenue when the currency weakens, India's regulatory environment for foreign companies can shift with limited notice, and local competition. Hindustan Unilever against Colgate, domestic fintech against Mastercard and Visa, is real and well-funded. None of these are disqualifying factors, but they are the variables that move the India revenue line beyond what the macro growth rate would predict.

MetricsHour tracks geographic revenue breakdowns for public companies, including the India segment where disclosed. The stock screener lets investors compare revenue exposure across geographies, useful for building a view on which companies carry genuine India revenue risk versus those with India as a cost center or a strategic aspiration not yet reflected in the numbers.

---

## Top Luxury Brands China Revenue 2026 Forecast
URL: https://metricshour.com/blog/luxury-brands-china-revenue-breakdown-2026
Date: 2026-03-28 | Category: data

LVMH generated roughly €22 billion in Asia ex-Japan revenue in 2023. Richemont disclosed China as its single largest market at 32–36% of group sales

LVMH generated roughly €22 billion in Asia ex-Japan revenue in 2023. Richemont disclosed China as its single largest market at 32–36% of group sales (FY2024). Hermès doesn't break out China at all. For investors tracking luxury brands' China revenue exposure, the data exists, but reading it correctly requires knowing where each company buries the number.

The Geographic Disclosure Problem

The lack of detailed China data is a persistent issue across the sector. LVMH reported Asia ex-Japan as approximately 28% of its €79 billion 2023 revenue, down from a peak of roughly 35% in 2021, but does not disaggregate China specifically. China is the dominant market within that segment, yet its precise share remains opaque. This makes it difficult to quantify LVMH's direct sensitivity to Chinese consumer trends or policy shifts.

Hermès takes the same approach. Its Asia Pacific ex-Japan segment represented about 48% of its €13.4 billion 2023 revenue, or approximately €5.7 billion. Hermès discloses no China-specific figure. Analyst estimates place China at roughly 30–35% of total revenue, but that is an inference, not a disclosure. The reliance on estimates introduces real uncertainty when stress-testing the brand's exposure to a Chinese demand slowdown.

This bundling practice contrasts sharply with the explicit disclosures from Richemont and Burberry, and it matters. When consumer behavior or regulation shifts specifically within China, investors in LVMH and Hermès cannot easily quantify the revenue impact against the group total.

Why China's Luxury Market Works Differently

China's luxury demand has structural features that separate it from Western markets. Purchases are predominantly made domestically. Chinese consumers buy in China, not abroad, which insulates the category from exchange rate movements and travel disruptions that affect duty-free and tourism-dependent luxury spend in Europe and Japan. The demand base skews younger and is aspirationally driven, with status and brand recognition carrying disproportionate weight.

The domestic purchase pattern has strengthened since 2020. Chinese authorities have cut import duties on luxury goods and expanded domestic duty-free zones, particularly in Hainan, partly to capture spend that previously flowed abroad through "daigou" grey-market channels. That policy shift has increased the addressable in-China market for luxury brands, even as it reduces the premium on cross-border price arbitrage that once drove tourism-related purchases.

This domestically-rooted demand also means the market responds more directly to Chinese macroeconomic conditions and government policy than to global cycles. GDP growth, consumer confidence, and regulatory signals from Beijing matter here in ways they don't for the same brands' European revenues. MetricsHour tracks China's macroeconomic indicators at China, providing context for the economic environment luxury brands are operating in.

Exposure Ranked: Who Carries the Most China Risk

The table below summarizes estimated and disclosed China revenue exposure across the five largest publicly listed luxury groups:

Brand
Ticker
China Exposure % 
Disclosure Method

Richemont
CFR.SW
32–36% (FY2024)
China specific 

Hermès
RMS.PA
30–35%
Analyst estimate within Asia Pacific ex-Japan

Kering
KER.PA
25–30%
Asia Pacific 

LVMH
MC.PA
~25–28%
Estimate within Asia ex-Japan segment

Burberry
BRBY.L
~20% (FY2025)
China specific

Richemont carries the highest explicitly disclosed China exposure among the group. At 32–36% of FY2024 group revenue, China is its largest single market, ahead of Europe or the Americas. That direct visibility is analytically useful but also shows Richemont's sensitivity to Chinese economic conditions and any shifts in high-end watch and jewelry demand.

Kering is most exposed at the brand level through Gucci, where China accounts for roughly 27–30% of revenue. Gucci's performance has been among the most affected in recent quarters as Chinese luxury spending moderated. Kering overall sits at approximately 25% China exposure. LVMH benefits from broader diversification, its 75 Maisons span wines and spirits, fashion, perfumes, watches, and retail, which blunts the impact of any single market's slowdown.

Hermès occupies a different position. Despite its large estimated China exposure, the brand's exceptional pricing power and consistent waitlists provide a demand floor that competitors cannot match. Burberry, the only other brand with explicit China disclosure at ~20% (FY2025), has faced the steepest recent pressure, a reminder that disclosure and resilience are separate questions.

Structural pressure and the Long-Term Case

Several factors complicate the China growth story as of 2025-2026. The government's "common prosperity" campaign remains an active policy priority, with ongoing pressure on conspicuous wealth displays. The initiative does not target luxury consumption directly, but its effect on consumer sentiment, particularly among the upper-middle class, has been measurable. Brands must address this through product positioning and pricing discipline rather than volume-driven growth.

The rise of domestic luxury and lifestyle brands adds a further layer of competition. Local players have grown in credibility among younger Chinese consumers, particularly in categories like jewelry, fashion accessories, and skincare. These brands combine nationalistic appeal with locally-tailored aesthetics, and their trajectory is upward. The post-COVID luxury rebound, while real, has proven less durable than initial demand signals suggested, pointing toward a more competitive and demand-sensitive market environment than existed in 2020–2021.

Despite these pressures, the structural case for China's luxury market remains intact. The consumer base is large, growing in wealth, and in earlier stages of luxury adoption than European counterparts. Brands with deep local retail networks. Hermès and LVMH foremost among them, have built positions that are difficult to displace. Early-mover advantages in city-tier penetration and brand recognition translate into durable revenue floors, even if the growth rate normalizes.

Tracking how these revenue breakdowns shift year over year is part of what MetricsHour is built for. The geographic revenue data for individual stocks is available through the MetricsHour stock screener, giving investors a consistent basis for comparing exposure levels across the sector as annual reports are updated.

---

## Tesla China Rev % 2026 TSLA Trends
URL: https://metricshour.com/blog/tesla-china-revenue-percentage-2026
Date: 2026-03-27 | Category: markets

Tesla China revenue is declining as a share of total revenue, a consistent trend from 2021-2024 that warrants close investor examination by early 2026.

Tesla's China revenue is declining, not in absolute dollars, but as a share of total revenue. The trend is consistent across four consecutive fiscal years, from FY2021 to FY2024, and shows no sign of reversing its trajectory as of early 2026. This shift warrants close examination by investors, as it indicates a rebalancing of Tesla's global revenue streams. The following data illustrates this pattern:

| Fiscal Year | Greater China Revenue | Total Revenue | China % of Total |
|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| FY2021 | ~$13.8B | ~$53.8B | ~25.6% |
| FY2022 | ~$18.1B | ~$81.5B | ~22.2% |
| FY2023 | ~$21.3B | ~$96.8B | ~22.0% |
| FY2024 | ~$21.0B | ~$97.7B | ~21.5% |

Source: Tesla 10-K SEC filings, Greater China segment, Macau, Taiwan.

Greater China's share of Tesla's total revenue has fallen by 4.1 percentage points, from 25.6% in FY2021 to 21.5% in FY2024. Absolute revenue from Greater China plateaued around $21 billion in FY2023 and FY2024, specifically $21.3 billion and $21.0 billion respectively. During this same period, Tesla's total revenue continued its upward trajectory, growing from $96.8 billion in FY2023 to $97.7 billion in FY2024. This divergence indicates that revenue growth in other geographic markets is outpacing that observed in Greater China. This represents a structural shift in Tesla's revenue composition, rather than a temporary fluctuation.

Why China Still Matters Even as the Percentage Falls

A declining revenue percentage does not mean Greater China is unimportant to Tesla. It means the opposite of what most investors assume: China is so embedded in Tesla's operations that its disruption would hurt globally, not just locally.

Gigafactory Shanghai is a global production hub, not solely a China factory. Model 3 and Model Y vehicles manufactured in Shanghai are shipped to Europe, Australia, and other markets, serving as a primary export base for Tesla's international operations. Giga Shanghai's output supports a significant portion of Tesla's worldwide delivery numbers and contributes to the company's overall production efficiency. The facility's advanced automation and supply chain integration provide cost advantages that benefit Tesla's global gross margins. Any disruption to operations in Shanghai, whether from trade restrictions, regulatory action, or labor issues, cascades directly into Tesla's ability to meet global demand and protect its profitability. Beyond manufacturing, China also serves as a critical market for battery supply chain development and software innovation, with local R&D teams adapting features for the Chinese consumer.

Greater China is also Tesla's second-largest single market by revenue, consistently after the United States. The approximately $21 billion generated there annually, as observed in FY2023 and FY2024, is not easily replaceable by growth in other regions in the near term. While the percentage trend matters for understanding where Tesla's growth is originating, the absolute revenue figure underscores the substantial financial contribution and strategic importance Tesla would forfeit if its presence in Greater China were to deteriorate sharply.

The Competitive Pressure Driving the Share Decline

Tesla entered China as a premium pioneer. That advantage has been compressed by a domestic EV industry that has closed the technology gap faster than almost any analyst predicted.

BYD is the most direct threat, having surpassed Tesla as the world's largest EV maker by volume in Q4 2023 and maintaining that position into 2026. BYD offers a full product lineup, from budget-friendly models like the Seagull to premium offerings under its Denza and Yangwang brands, and possesses significant vertical integration advantages in battery production. Its international ambitions are growing, with expansion into Europe and Southeast Asia. Li Auto, NIO, and Xpeng have each carved out strong positions in segments that Tesla once dominated unchallenged. Li Auto specializes in range-extended electric vehicles (EREVs) and large family SUVs, appealing to consumers seeking long-distance travel without range anxiety. NIO focuses on premium services, battery swap technology, and a strong community brand. Xpeng emphasizes advanced driver-assistance systems (ADAS) and intelligent cockpit features, often tailored to Chinese consumer preferences for sophisticated in-car technology.

Tesla's response to this intense competition has been primarily focused on price adjustments. Multiple rounds of price cuts in Greater China since 2023 have been implemented to maintain sales volume, but these actions have pressured revenue per vehicle and compressed profit margins in the region. The mass market segment, for vehicles priced below 300,000 RMB, is now effectively ceded to domestic competitors who offer a wider array of models and features at lower price points. This segment includes popular models from BYD, NETA, and Leapmotor.

Where Tesla still holds a significant position is the premium segment, for vehicles priced above 300,000 RMB, where it commands approximately 30% market share as of early 2026. Chinese buyers in this tier continue to value the Tesla brand, the extensive Supercharger network, and the integrated software experience in a way that domestic alternatives haven't yet fully replicated. However, this premium segment is a smaller and increasingly contested slice of the overall EV market than it was in 2024. New entrants like Zeekr, AITO (backed by Huawei), and premium offerings from traditional automakers are intensifying competition even in this higher-margin category.

The Musk Wildcard

No analysis of Tesla's China outlook is complete without acknowledging the Elon Musk variable. His political profile, and particularly his relationship with US government policy, creates brand and regulatory risk in Greater China that no other major foreign automaker faces to the same degree.

Chinese consumers and government officials are sensitive to perceived political alignment and national interests. A shift in Musk's public posture toward China, or an escalation in US-China tensions tied to technology, trade, or tariffs, could translate into various forms of pressure. This might include boycott risk from consumers, increased regulatory scrutiny of Tesla's data collection and storage practices within China, or delays in approval for new models and software features. For example, concerns over data security led to restrictions on Tesla vehicles in certain government compounds in 2021.

This risk is not hypothetical; other foreign brands have faced targeted pressure in China during periods of diplomatic friction. Tesla's size, high visibility, and association with a prominent American figure make it a plausible target if the geopolitical environment deteriorates further. While investors cannot quantify this risk precisely, it remains an important consideration in any honest assessment of the Greater China revenue outlook and Tesla's long-term operational stability in the region.

What to Watch in 2026

Premium segment market share. If Tesla's approximately 30% share in the above-300,000 RMB tier begins to erode significantly, that would serve as a leading indicator of broader brand weakness or intensified competition in Greater China. Investors should closely monitor quarterly delivery data from domestic premium EV makers such as NIO, Li Auto, Zeekr, and AITO for signs they are encroaching on this segment with compelling new models or enhanced service offerings.

Giga Shanghai export volume vs. Domestic sales split. Tesla does not explicitly break out Giga Shanghai's export volume versus domestic sales within China in its financial reports. However, shipping data from ports and regional delivery announcements provide reasonable proxies. If domestic Greater China sales flatten or decline while exports from Shanghai hold up or increase, the reported Greater China revenue figure would understate the region's actual strategic importance as a global manufacturing and export base for Tesla.

New model timing. Tesla's next-generation affordable vehicle, which began initial production in 2025, is now competing in China's highly competitive mass market segment. The timing and scale of its production ramp-up at Giga Shanghai are critical. If Giga Shanghai becomes the primary production site for this entry-level model, and it achieves strong sales volumes domestically, Greater China's revenue contribution could potentially reverse its declining trend and regain percentage share. Its success will depend on competitive pricing, feature sets, and local market acceptance against established domestic rivals.

US-China policy escalation. Any new round of tech export controls, tariffs on Chinese-made EV components, or retaliatory measures from Beijing targeting foreign automakers would affect Tesla's Greater China operations directly and quickly. Potential scenarios include increased import duties on Tesla vehicles sold in the US (if produced in China), restrictions on key component exports, or heightened regulatory hurdles for Tesla's data handling and autonomous driving software in China. These policy shifts could force Tesla to re-evaluate its supply chain and production strategies.

The declining percentage of Tesla's total revenue derived from Greater China is a clear trend observed from FY2021 to FY2024. This shift reflects intensified domestic competition and Tesla's strategic adjustments, including price reductions to maintain volume. Despite this percentage decline, Greater China remains an important market for Tesla, both as a significant revenue generator and as a global manufacturing and export hub through Gigafactory Shanghai. Investors should monitor Tesla's premium segment market share, the domestic versus export split from Giga Shanghai, the performance of its new affordable vehicle in China, and the evolving geopolitical dynamic between the US and China throughout 2026. These factors will determine whether Greater China stabilizes its revenue contribution or continues its proportional decline within Tesla's global financial picture.

Track Tesla's geographic revenue breakdown, updated from the latest SEC filings, at Tesla. See how China compares to Tesla's other markets and monitor the trend as new filings land.

---

## Revenue Country Stock Screener: Alternatives
URL: https://metricshour.com/blog/stock-screener-by-revenue-country
Date: 2026-03-27 | Category: markets

You can screen stocks by P/E, revenue growth, and 200+ variables. Geographic revenue screening is the missing tool for tariff-era investing.

You can screen stocks by P/E ratio, revenue growth, short interest, dividend yield, sector, market cap, and 200 other variables. You cannot screen them by geographic revenue.

Try it. Open Finviz, Yahoo Finance, Koyfin, Morningstar, or TIKR and find the filter that shows you all S&P 500 companies deriving less than 5% of revenue from China. It isn't there. None of them have it.

This is the single most important missing feature in stock research tools, and in 2026, it matters more than ever.

What Each Major Screener Can and Can't Do

To be precise about the gap:

Finviz is excellent for valuations, technical filters, and sector screens. You can filter by sales growth, gross margin, P/S ratio, and dozens of others. Geographic revenue breakdown: not available.

Yahoo Finance covers earnings estimates, analyst ratings, and basic fundamentals well. Its screener spans many metrics. Country-level revenue exposure: not available.

Koyfin is built for professionals and offers financial statement data, estimates, and clean visualizations. You can screen on margin trends, debt levels, and earnings revisions. Filter by percentage of revenue from India: not available.

Morningstar is the authority on fund analysis and equity research. Its fundamental screener is thorough. Identify all stocks with zero Russia exposure post-2022 sanctions: not available.

TIKR provides deep financial data and is one of the best tools for reading individual company filings. You can find geographic revenue for a single company by digging into its 10-K notes. Screen an entire index by this metric: not possible.

The pattern holds across every platform: geographic revenue is not a filterable dimension in any mainstream screener.

Why This Data Has Never Been Standardized

The reason isn't laziness. It's structural. Geographic revenue isn't a standardized line item on an income statement. It lives in the footnotes of a company's 10-K filing, typically buried in "Segment Information" or "Geographic Information" notes, using whatever regional groupings that company chose to report. Apple calls it "Greater China." Others report "Asia Pacific" or "Rest of World" without breaking out individual countries.

Extracting this data requires reading each company's filing, identifying the relevant table, and mapping inconsistent geographic categories to a common schema. Multiply that across 500 companies, updated quarterly, and you understand why no general-purpose screener has committed to solving it. It requires a dedicated data layer, not a bolt-on feature.

Why This Gap Is Urgent in 2026

Three forces have made geographic revenue the most under-served dimension in investor research:

Tariffs and trade escalation. US tariff rates on Chinese goods are at multi-decade highs. A company that looks solid on core fundamentals can carry 40% China revenue, making its earnings directly exposed to policy that has nothing to do with its products or management quality. Investors cannot price this risk without the data.

Decoupling and friend-shoring. Both governments are actively restructuring which countries can access which technology. For semiconductor companies in particular, the question isn't "does this company operate well?" but "does this company's customer base sit behind an export control boundary?" That's a geographic revenue question.

Growth opportunity mapping. The flip side of risk is opportunity. India's GDP is growing at approximately 7% annually. Vietnam, Mexico, and Indonesia are absorbing supply chain shifts from China. Investors who can identify S&P 500 companies with the highest revenue exposure to these markets have a structural edge, if they can screen for them.

Five Things You Could Do With a Country-Revenue Screener

1. Build a tariff-resilient portfolio. Screen for S&P 500 stocks with the lowest China revenue. Companies like Lockheed Martin derive approximately 0% of revenue from China, their customers are the US government and allied militaries. That's structural insulation from US-China trade friction, not luck.

2. Find the most India-exposed S&P 500 companies. India's growth is real and durable. Which US companies are already positioned to benefit from it? A country-revenue screen answers this immediately across all 500 stocks simultaneously, not one 10-K at a time.

3. Map semiconductor China exposure before it becomes a headline. Qualcomm derives ~46% of revenue from China; ASML ~49%. Both carry meaningful regulatory risk. Knowing this before an export control announcement, not after, is the entire point.

4. Identify sanctions exposure before a geopolitical event. Companies with Russia revenue had to disclose and write down those assets after 2022. Investors who knew the exposure beforehand could act on it. The same logic applies to any country where geopolitical risk is building.

5. Construct a domestic-only portfolio. Utilities, regional banks, and defense contractors generate the vast majority of their revenue inside the US. A country-revenue screener isolates these companies cleanly, no manual review required.

How MetricsHour Fills the Gap

MetricsHour was built specifically around the geographic revenue data layer that mainstream screeners ignore.

The China shows every tracked stock ranked by its China revenue percentage, highest to lowest, so you can immediately see which companies are most and least exposed. The same structure exists for India, Germany, Japan, and every other major market.

Each individual stock page shows the full geographic breakdown from the company's SEC filings: what percentage of revenue comes from each region, and how that has shifted over recent reporting periods.
